text:
, Homunculus (, Homunkurusu), later in life referred to as Father (お父様, Otō-sama), is the main antagonist of the Fullmetal Alchemist manga and 2009 anime series. He was the oldest homunculus and the progenitor of the homunculi, having created them directly or indirectly. Arrogant and callous, he considered only power, dominion over the world, "perfect goodness and being", and "divine order" to be worthy of his attention. His ultimate goal was to become absolutely superior to the human race. His main form bears a striking resemblance to Van Hohenheim.

Father was created from the essence of the Eye, with some of Hohenheim's blood mixed in, and as a result, his original black gaseous form closely resembles the Eye itself. Father inherited vast knowledge from the Eye, and in terms of sheer scope, only the Eye and Truth surpass him.

Personality

Father's original personality, when he was known as the Dwarf in the Flask, was far more humanlike and even charming compared to his later self. He was polite, charismatic, and at times sarcastic, traits that Van Hohenheim described as being "full of human emotions." Although he was much more verbose than he would be later in life, he still had hints of a general superiority complex against humans, aware of his advanced knowledge in comparison to them despite his inferior position.

Father was highly intelligent, manipulative, callous, and narcissistic, though outwardly he portrayed himself much more stoically, appearing as a composed and almost lifeless old man, even appearing bored. Although he could feign energy and friendliness if he wanted to, he treated nearly every other person with absolute disregard and hardly showed emotion to even his own "children". This lack of emotion was attributed to his decision to purge himself of the seven vices. However, this "purging" appears to have been incomplete, as Father continued to act in ways that reflected those very sins.

Over time, especially when his plans became closer to fruition, his stoic demeanor would show cracks to a more manic, energetic personality. He was shown to be visibly excited when the Day of Reckoning arrived, as well as showing much more straightforward cruelty and anger towards his enemies whereas before there would be indifference.

Father had a complex and often contradictory relationship with humans. Outwardly, he maintained that he believed that humans were obviously inferior to him and the Homunculi, and regarded them as weak, stupid, and easily manipulated, akin to insects to him. He firmly believed that they only served as pawns to be used for his own ends and did not bother to try to understand them, seeing it as pointless. On the other hand, Hohenheim suggested that the entire reason he created the Homunculi was because he desired a family of his own, a statement that was not denied. This was supported by the fact that he did treat them in a somewhat familial way, the entire name "Father" being one he had them call him.

Despite this, his relationship with his supposed "children" was stoic at best, although the most positive relationships he had. When Gluttony was near death, he revived him with his memories intact, and even after Greed betrayed him he was given a chance to rejoin them. That being said, when the time came to it, he showed no regard or remorse at the deaths of the Homunculi, even choosing to abandon them when they no longer seemed useful.

Although he portrayed himself as omniscient and all-knowing, internally he was obsessed with knowledge and the idea of perfection. Father viewed the Truth as a personal God, and believed that by absorbing this "God", he would become a perfect being. Despite his immense knowledge, it was still shown that he too was ignorant of the ways of the Truth and did not fully understand the forces he contended with.

Appearance and Abilities

True Form

Initial

Father's first form is a small, round mass of black gas with a single eye, a wide toothy mouth, and tendril arms, making him resemble the Eye of Truth from inside the Gate. This may be because Father was created from that entity’s essence. As the "Dwarf in the Flask", he was unable to perform alchemy himself, but his immense knowledge of it and general understanding of the world allowed him to instruct and guide the people of Xerxes. In this form he was very vulnerable and stated that he would die if he left the flask. After his final defeat, he reverts to this original form and is soon assimilated by the Eye of God.

Container

Due to his imitation of Hohenheim’s body in Xerxes, Father’s second form is a vessel that looks nearly identical to Hohenheim, differing only in hair and skin color, hairstyle, clothing, a few additional wrinkles, and the absence of glasses. Hohenheim described this form as nothing more than a humanlike husk that Father uses to conceal his true appearance. Father is usually seen in the chamber beneath Central, where he and the other homunculi reside, seated upon a Philosopher’s Stone or cement throne connected to numerous pipes. The throne itself is physically linked to him, with several pipes protruding from his spine that can be detached. He uses these pipes to channel the Philosopher’s Stone throughout the nation of Amestris. This system serves as a barrier between the tectonic energy of the earth’s crust, the source of Amestrian alchemy, allowing Father to halt the use of alchemy at will. However, maintaining this system has caused his body to age slightly more than Van Hohenheim’s, who still appears the same as he did during the time of Xerxes. This process can be countered by a "Reverse Transmutation Circle", a concept developed by Scar's brother through the combination of Xingese alkahestry and Amestrian alchemy practices. Alkahestry remains unaffected by Father's barrier because it uses energy from the Earth's chi rather than from tectonic energy. This allows Scar and May Chang to continue using their alchemy even when Father blocks Amestrian alchemy.

Father’s body was created from, and contains, the Philosopher’s Stone composed of half the souls of the human population of Xerxes. He later used portions of this Stone to create the other homunculi. Father has also demonstrated the ability to produce Philosopher’s Stones directly from his body, as well as absorb them. Because of his origins as a being from The Gate, Father possesses a level of knowledge in alchemical theory and practice far beyond that of any other being on Earth. Combined with the nearly limitless energy drawn from the souls of Xerxes, he can perform alchemy without drawing a transmutation circle, clapping his hands, or even moving. In this form, Father created his seven sin-based homunculi, granting each of them unique and powerful abilities such as the Ultimate Spear, Ultimate Shield, and Ultimate Eye. As a homunculus himself, Father possesses the enhanced physical capabilities typical of his creations, though his strength far exceeds theirs, since their Philosopher’s Stones are only fragments of his own. If he chooses, he can reabsorb his homunculi by liquefying their Philosopher’s Stones and consuming them.

Despite his immense knowledge and understanding of alchemy, Father was neither all-knowing nor all-powerful. There were times when he failed in his experiments, such as during the creation of Gluttony, or when he was caught off guard by the unexpected properties of the Philosopher’s Stone itself. His unending pursuit of greater knowledge served as proof that there were still many things even he did not understand.

Evolved

During his battle with Hohenheim, Hohenheim initially gains the upper hand. However, after Father discards his "second form" husk and reveals his true form, which has evolved to exist outside of a container, an unseen clash occurs between them that ends in Father’s victory. In this form, Father possesses a strange, amorphous body structure that faintly resembles his ancient flask form. He can use alchemy to reshape his body to suit any situation. The form he most often assumes is that of a slightly obese humanoid with red eyes scattered across his black body and a wide grin on his otherwise featureless face.

While in this form, Father can assume an appearance identical to that of his son Pride, though he does not require a container to maintain it. Unlike Pride’s solid shadow form, Father’s body has a more fluid and gelatinous consistency. When Greed attempted to strike him, Father simply dissolved into a puddle and reformed at a distance.

When Father absorbs around fifty million souls from the people of Amestris, his form changes into that of a gigantic, cyclopean being. Father had previously implied, in a cryptic manner, that this was his true form all along, although it was once much smaller, confined only to his chamber, in order to discourage his sacrifices from attempting escape. In this new form, he appears much thinner, with a visible rib cage and a more muscular physique. The numerous eyes and wide grins that once covered his body are gone, replaced by a single large, bulging eye on his forehead.

After absorbing God

Father's fourth form closely resembles a younger version of Hohenheim. Despite his youthful appearance, he retains the voice of his second form. Through the sacrifices he made, Father was able to absorb the entity beyond the Gate and gain the "power of God" within himself. The Amestrian souls he absorbed serve to keep this immense power in check, making him a so-called "perfect being". In this state, he can almost completely defy the natural order, or as he describes it, "do anything". He demonstrates abilities far beyond those of any other alchemist in the series, capable of manipulating the weather, performing multiple complex transmutations simultaneously, and generating any force at any magnitude. 

He retains his fourth form and the powers that accompany it even after losing the Amestrian souls. However, without their strength to contain the "God" within him, the power becomes too great a strain on his body. In chapter 107, this weakness leaves him vulnerable to a counterattack. As the battle progresses, Father’s appearance deteriorates: his skin and hair lose their color, veins become pronounced across his body, and his face gradually loses nearly all human resemblance. At first, he can generate a form of "force field" that protects him without physical movement, but he eventually becomes unable to sustain it as his control weakens.

Chapter 106 also reveals that Father gains the ability to create bodies for the souls within his Philosopher’s Stone. In the manga, these creations are unstable and begin to fall apart shortly after being formed. Father uses this ability to distract his opponents so that he can prepare a devastating attack.

However, Father's obsession with becoming a god turns his powers into a double-edged sword. Once deprived of the Amestrian souls, he is unable to activate his invisible shield and his absorption abilities simultaneously. Moreover, his reliance on alchemy-based combat leaves him defenseless when his use of alchemy threatens the stability of the "power of God" he has absorbed. In this weakened state, he possesses virtually no hand-to-hand combat skill.

Part in the Story

While it was never fully explored, the being that would later call itself Father was originally a part of the existence within the Gate. An alchemist working for the King of Xerxes succeeded in containing a fragment of the Gate’s knowledge and life within a flask, allowing it to exist in the physical realm. To accomplish this, the alchemist used some blood from of one of his slaves, a young man known then only as Number 23. The alchemist's creation came to be known as the "Dwarf in the Flask" or "Homunculus" by the people of Xerxes. This sentient creature possessed immense knowledge, most notably of the foundational principles that would later form the basis of the alchemy of Amestris.

The Dwarf in the Flask was unable to perform any alchemy on his own. Furthermore, though the flask was the only thing keeping him alive, the Homunculus longed to exist beyond it. To that end, he befriended Slave 23, finding in him a kindred spirit, for both were slaves to their respective plights. In gratitude for the part the young man played in his creation, the Homunculus gave him a name, Van Hohenheim, and educated him so that he might earn his freedom.

However, the Homunculus eventually grew envious of humanity, jealous of the emotional bonds they shared while he remained isolated, the only one of his kind. Losing sight of his own unique and extraordinary nature, he began to despise his existence. This insecurity festered into arrogance, and his wounded pride gave rise to a consuming ambition to surpass even God himself. 

Years later, the King of Xerxes, who like Father had grown vain and hubristic, finally revealed his purpose for the Homunculus: he wished to obtain the secret of immortality. The Homunculus seized this opportunity and instructed the king to construct a vast transmutation circle encompassing the entire kingdom. When the circle was activated, every soul in Xerxes, except for himself and Hohenheim, was sacrificed to create what was likely the first Philosopher's Stone.

The Homunculus used this power to open the Gate of Truth and created new, immortal bodies for himself and Hohenheim, living embodiments of the Philosopher’s Stone, each sustained by hundreds of thousands of souls taken from the people of Xerxes. Horrified by his role as an unwilling participant in this atrocity, Hohenheim parted ways with the Homunculus.

After the destruction of Xerxes, the Homunculus set out in search of another inhabited place. Upon finding a small group of people thriving there, he taught them the art of alchemy and became known as the "Philosopher of the East". However, to ensure his teachings could not be used against him, he placed himself under what would become Central Command and used himself as a buffer against tectonic energy so alchemists would actually use energy derived from the souls of Xerxes contained within himself. Eventually, he began using those same souls to create his homunculi offspring and thereafter came to be known as "Father".

From this point, the people established the powerful military state of Amestris, a puppet government secretly controlled by Father. Under his influence, Amestris rose in power and began annexing surrounding territories, eventually expanding to form yet another massive transmutation circle. All military conflicts and bloodshed orchestrated by the Amestrian army occurred at key nodes of this circle. Father’s ultimate goal was to recreate his actions at Xerxes to forge another Philosopher’s Stone, but this time he required an eclipse to achieve his transformation into a “perfect being”. As Edward Elric later explains, “the sun symbolizes a man, and the moon symbolizes a woman; when they come together, they become a perfect being”. To complete the ritual, Father also needed five alchemists who had opened the Gate as human sacrifices.

Throughout the first half of the story, Father is depicted seated upon a throne made of Philosopher’s Stone, surrounded by numerous pipes. He is formally introduced when Wrath captures Greed and brings him to Father’s underground lair. After Greed rejects Father’s offer to rejoin him, Father has him boiled in a vat of liquid that decomposes his body into its core Philosopher’s Stone. Now reduced to a liquid state containing Greed’s soul, the Philosopher’s Stone is then consumed by Father.

Father's full identity is revealed when Gluttony brings Alphonse into the homunculi's lair, shortly before Gluttony's body tears apart as Envy, Edward and Ling Yao emerge from within him. When the Elrics recognize his face, Father expresses amazement that Edward and Alphonse are Hohenheim’s children, then proceeds to heal Edward’s wounds and restore Alphonse’s missing arm. Having no use for Ling, however, Father orders Gluttony and Envy to kill him. Edward pleads for Ling’s life, but Father makes it clear that he cares for no one who is not part of his plan. When Edward and Alphonse attack him using alchemy, their efforts are completely ineffective. Father remains unharmed and easily neutralizes their power by stomping his foot to seal off all alchemy in Central.

At that moment, Scar and May Chang enter the lair and astonish Father with counterattacks that remain unaffected by his alchemic lockout, thanks to their unique forms of alchemy. Even at point-blank range, however, Scar’s alchemy fails to harm Father. Scar and May are soon overwhelmed while Envy captures the Elric brothers. Meanwhile, Father easily defeats Ling, takes the liquefied Philosopher’s Stone containing Greed’s soul, and merges it with Ling, remarking that he could make use of another pawn after all. Ling then becomes the new Greed, and Father orders the Elrics to be taken to the surface before tending to the injured Gluttony. From that point onward, Father is mostly seen seated on his throne, contemplating his plan and conversing with the military high command.

The Promised Day

When the Promised Day begins, Edward, Hohenheim, Scar, Lan Fan, and the chimeras invade Father’s lair with the intent to destroy him once and for all. However, only Hohenheim manages to reach him, and the two engage in battle. When it appears that Father has gained the upper hand after stabbing Hohenheim, he soon realizes too late that Hohenheim had collaborated with the souls within his Philosopher’s Stone, some of which begin to destroy Father’s shell from the inside. Unfortunately for Hohenheim, Father demonstrates how far he has evolved since their separation, emerging from his damaged shell and consuming it along with the Philosopher’s Stones it still contained before resuming the fight. He eventually defeats Hohenheim, restrains him with his own body, and later greets Izumi and the Elric brothers, who at first do not recognize him. Shortly afterward, Pride arrives with a blinded Colonel Mustang, providing Father with his fifth and final human sacrifice.

Despite the alchemists' refusal to aid him and Greed's attempt on his life, Father defeats his attackers and activates the Nationwide Transmutation Circle around Amestris. He uses the restrained sacrifices’ Gates to access the Earth’s Gate while absorbing the souls of all Amestrian citizens. In his complete form, Father tears open the Gate of Truth with his bare hands and absorbs the higher power he calls "God". Once the transmutation is complete, he emerges in a new youthful body and declares to the alchemists that he has “imprisoned God” within himself and now possesses all divine knowledge and power. No longer requiring sacrifices, Father cancels alchemy once more and prepares to eliminate the five sacrifices, along with Greed and May, by creating a miniature sun in the palm of his hand.

Final Battle and Defeat

Hohenheim then reveals his own plan: the souls of the people of Xerxes, which he separated from his body and merged with the earth itself, use the moon’s shadow as a circle to force Father to release the Amestrian souls and return them to their bodies. Without those souls, Father begins to lose control of “God”, and his body becomes covered with bulging veins from the strain. Enraged, Father claims he can always create another Philosopher’s Stone using hundreds of millions or even billions of lives, since there will always be more humans to draw energy from. He attacks by forming a massive sphere of energy and launching it, which Hohenheim blocks. Father launches another wave of energy at his human foes, but May transmutes an alkahestric barrier around them, declaring that her craft specializes in harnessing the flow of energy within the earth and that the more energy is thrown at her, the more she can redirect. However, when Father fires another volley, Hohenheim steps in to block it with his hands, which burn and crumble as it becomes clear that he lacks the energy to compete with Father. As he begins to fall back, Ed and Al rush to support him, allowing him to continue fighting. Their grateful father redoubles his efforts, straining his Philosopher’s Stone. Scar then activates the alkahestric reverse Nationwide Transmutation Circle, which nullifies Father’s buffer and restores true alchemy to the Amestrians. Ed, Al, and Izumi continue their assault while Hohenheim explains that they can win if they can force the homunculus to exhaust all the energy of his Philosopher’s Stone. Greed emerges from the shadows to join the fight, but Father deflects their attacks and ascends toward the surface. Knowing that continued combat will only weaken his ability to contain “God” within him, Father rises to the surface of Central Command to claim the souls of nearby Briggs soldiers and replenish his Philosopher’s Stone. However, a surprise attack from Hohenheim disrupts his concentration.

Now surrounded by Hohenheim, Izumi, Alphonse, and May, Father is asked by Hohenheim why he hates humans. Hohenheim points out that Father’s origins and the very existence of the homunculi stem from Philosopher’s Stones, which are created from human souls. Hohenheim asserts that Father is capable only of destruction and that his so-called evolution has reached a dead end. Father replies that he can create and proves Hohenheim wrong by using his body to resurrect the people of Xerxes. As the alchemists recoil in horror, the revived citizens of Xerxes wander aimlessly, grateful to have returned to the world of the living. At that moment, Edward finally makes his way to the surface. Seeing him, Father grins and releases a massive energy blast that annihilates both the resurrected people of Xerxes and most of Central Command. Al and Hohenheim manage to shield everyone, but at great cost to their own bodies. Ed looks at his father’s scorched form and calls out to him, but as Hohenheim opens his eyes, Father appears behind him and swats him aside. Turning his attention to Izumi and Edward as his next targets, Father attempts to draw out their souls to forge another Philosopher’s Stone. However, he is interrupted when the military arrives to join the battle, with the blind Mustang supported by Lieutenant Riza Hawkeye and Major Alex Louis Armstrong leading the charge.

The Briggs soldiers, pouring out from Central Command, open fire on Father with rifles, rockets, and machine guns, buying the alchemists time to retreat to safety. Father merely shrugs off the attacks, shielding himself with an impenetrable barrier of alchemical energy. The soldiers are then ordered to stand clear as a torrent of flame engulfs their inhuman foe. Though Father remains unharmed, Colonel Mustang, Major Armstrong, and Lieutenant Hawkeye have arrived on the scene. Hawkeye informs the blind Mustang that his aim was off and gives him targeting directions for his next massive salvo. Clapping his hands together to compensate for the damage to his flame alchemy gloves, Roy remarks that he has not yet mastered circle-free transmutation and attacks Father with flames once again. Father simply absorbs the fire and reflects it back at him. Warned by Hawkeye, Mustang quickly transmutes a wall to defend against the counterattack and finds the clapping technique useful.

Despite their combined efforts, Father remains unscathed and declares that humans are incapable of breaching his defenses. Suddenly, Greed bursts from the cloud of dust and gun smoke and lands a punch squarely on his father’s face, proclaiming that a homunculus might succeed where humans have failed. As Father grabs Greed’s hand, he thanks his son for delivering a complete Philosopher’s Stone at the moment he needed it most and begins draining its energy. However, Greed counters that he anticipated this and reveals that his plan is to drain the godly energy instead. Greed begins siphoning power from his father’s body, but Father’s pull is too strong, and he reverses the flow. Seeing their comrade in danger, Edward, Armstrong, and Izumi rush to Greed’s aid, unleashing a volley of physical attacks. Overwhelmed by the combined onslaught, Father releases a burst of energy that hurls them all backward, shattering Edward’s automail arm in the process. Undeterred, the alchemists launch a second wave. When Father blocks a kick from Edward with his arm instead of alchemical energy, Hohenheim realizes that Father is nearing his limit and can barely keep “God” contained within his body. Writhing in agony as God’s eye rises from his gullet and into his mouth, threatening to escape, Father collapses to the ground and releases a massive surge of energy that throws everyone away from him.

Barely managing to keep “God” contained within his form, Father frantically searches for a soul to convert into a Philosopher’s Stone. Spotting Edward sprawled across a pile of broken concrete, he moves desperately to take Fullmetal’s soul and replenish his dwindling supply. Seeing the danger as Father advances, Ed tries to crawl to safety but realizes his left arm has been impaled on a piece of twisted rebar jutting from the rubble, trapping him in place. He is saved, however, by Al, who with May’s help sacrifices himself to restore Ed’s original right arm. A distraught Edward calls Alphonse an idiot before wrenching himself free and launching a furious barrage of attacks against Father, greatly depleting the homunculus’s Philosopher’s Stone.

On the verge of defeat, Father reels in shock at being bested by a mere human. Another powerful blow from Ed sends the homunculus crashing to the ground, but when the godly energy attempts to escape again, Father absorbs it, rises to his feet, and lunges desperately at Greed to take his Philosopher’s Stone from Ling’s body. As he plunges his arm into his son’s stomach and begins draining the Stone, Ling’s consciousness grabs hold of Greed within their shared body, refusing to let him go. Yet, as Greed notes, Father’s pull is stronger, and any further struggle will only result in Ling’s soul being consumed as well. When Ling insists that he needs Greed’s power to become Emperor of Xing, Greed claims to accept the partnership, but at the last moment knocks him away, resigning himself to his father’s summons. The avaricious homunculus declares that this was the only lie he has ever told, assuring Ling that he can still become emperor since Lan Fan holds the Philosopher’s Stone she took from Wrath’s pocket. As Father draws out his son’s Stone, his arm turns gray while he absorbs Greed’s carbon armor ability. In his final moments within Ling’s body, Greed calls out to Lan Fan, who rushes in and severs Father’s arm with a single slash of her kunai. Ling collapses to the ground, restored to full humanity as the Ouroboros mark on his hand fades away, while Father howls in anguish at his son’s betrayal. Though his entire body slowly turns to carbon under Greed’s power, the avaricious homunculus explains from within that he has used his armor ability to transform Father’s body into the weakest, most fragile form of carbon imaginable. Father’s entire being begins to crumble under its own weight. Enraged that his own son would rebel against him to the very end, Father destroys Greed’s soul. However, ensuring Greed's death was not in vain, Edward takes this opportunity to punch a hole through Father's chest, allowing the remaining Xerxesian souls of his Philosopher’s Stone to escape. With no power or souls left to contain “God” or to sustain his existence, Father watches in horror as “God” emerges from his body and grabs him. Father laments the cruelty of reality, crying out that he cannot understand why it denies him his greatest desires and why true freedom is forever out of reach. Moments later, his body vanishes.

Now returned to his original form, Father appears before the Gate and asks God why he was not accepted as its vessel. He receives a reply: "It is because you did not believe in yourself". Searching for the source of the voice, Father encounters Truth, unaware of its identity. Truth mocks him, saying that Father stole his power from others and that, though born of man, he clung to what he called God without ever truly growing. It ridicules him for believing that he could surpass humans by severing himself from his seven desires. Father then asks what was wrong with wanting, wishing, and pursuing one’s desires, revealing the very humanity he had tried so desperately to purge. Demanding to know who he is speaking to, Father is horrified when Truth formally introduces itself, revealing that it is Truth as he once defined it—one that brings despair to discourage humans from becoming boastful. Truth tells Father that he will now suffer the same despair as the Gate opens and hands reach out to seize him. Father cries that he does not want to return to where he came from, desperately asking what he did wrong to deserve this and where he went astray. Ultimately, stripped of the freedom he cherished, Father is dragged into the Gate and becomes part of the endless abyss once more.

Relationships

Homunculi

Father's relationship with his so-called "children" was relatively cold and uncaring. He never showed any form of affection or real emotion to any of them, only addressing them in his calm, if not bored, tone. As their creator, he expected undying loyalty from the homunculi and did not hesitate to melt the first Greed (though he did ask to rejoin him) into a Philosopher's Stone as punishment for betraying him. Even though the homunculi were on a whole completely obedient and loyal to him, he did not have much particular allegiance to them in return. When Pride was left vulnerable after exhausting his own Philosopher’s Stone, Father paid him no mind and simply chose to abandon him. Still, Hohenheim observed that Father, in his own twisted way, made some attempt at forging a familial connection with his creations, having them call him their “father”, while he, in turn, referred to them as his “children”.

Van Hohenheim

Father and Hohenheim initially shared a friendly relationship. Father acted as a partner, mentor, and friend to Hohenheim, and was instrumental in raising him from his status as a slave. Father claimed he did this out of gratitude for having been born from Hohenheim’s blood, further rewarding him with both a name and immortality. However, after the fall of Xerxes, their relationship deteriorated into bitter enmity. Hohenheim devoted his life to stopping Father’s ambitions, while Father came to see Hohenheim merely as another potential human sacrifice and a source of Philosopher’s Stones. Although Father treated his former companion with contempt and indifference, Hohenheim’s feelings were closer to pity. He believed that Father’s obsession with becoming a perfect being concealed his true, unacknowledged desire to be more like humans.

Trivia

, when Edward is reading a book about the Philosopher's Stone]] 
Father's original flask-bound form was most likely inspired by real-life concepts of homunculi. Supposedly, if an alchemist was successful in creating such a being, he was to keep it inside of a glass flask for a certain period of time, as it would die in its "infancy" stage outside of it. Father's evolved multi-eyed and multi-mouthed form and its giant incarnation could be a play on the "homunculus argument" fallacy.
Both Fullmetal Alchemist: Brotherhood and the latter half of the manga of Soul Eater were developed by Studio Bones, inspiring several new characters in the series, one of which being Table of Contents, a mysterious character that has numerous similarities to Father, acting as the leader of a group of characters based on the seven deadly sins, and was created by another character infatuated with the path of knowledge.
Father and Envy from the 2003 anime share several similarities, as both have some sort of previous affiliation with Hohenheim, being created by him. The two were considered the first homunculi in their respective storylines, being approximately 400 years old. It is also interesting to note that Envy's original form resembles Father's fourth form, with both looking like younger versions of Hohenheim.
Though it is been stated numerous times that Father has expelled all of his sins, it is interesting to note he acts in a way similar to that of the sins: He desires for becoming a perfect being (Lust); devours souls with no self-control (Gluttony); seeks power at all costs (Greed); prefers to let others do his bidding for him while being incredibly incompetent and doing little to nothing to further his own goals over hundreds of years (Sloth); feels anger towards any opposition, principally Hohenheim (Wrath); has a desire for a family like humans (Envy); views humans as "ants" (Pride). However, given the general vagueness of these behaviors, as well as his dispassionate demeanor compared to both prior to the creation of and after the death of the homunculi, seems to imply that he did get himself purged of his sins.
Indeed, the process of rejecting his sins is hinted by the Truth to be the reason for his malevolence.
He has told Greed that anything he wanted was desired by him first, meaning that Greed's desire for friends was something Father desired but expelled because he thought it was a weakness.
When Father succeeded in opening the Gate, he emerged from the ground in a much larger form of himself. This massive form of Father is exactly the same as his previous form yet his body is slightly altered. He is much thinner, showing what looks like a rib cage, a more muscular body, the multitude of eyes and wide grins that were on his previous form are gone, and he now has one large, bulging eye on his forehead.
Father's image bears a striking resemblance with the image concept of God as an old man with robes in multiple religions. Ironically, the nature and symbolism of his origin, methods, and goals actually make him the closest analog the Fullmetal Alchemist franchise has to Satan. Dr. Tim Marcoh referring to the Philosopher's Stone as "the Devil's research" is found to hint at this when it turns out that Father is responsible for the first stones ever created and seeks to achieve "godhood" by further powering his own stone with souls.

Father's voice actor in both the Japanese and English version also narrates the 2009 anime. They can be heard previewing the next episode and at the beginning of the first few episodes where they briefly explain the concept of alchemy to the viewers.

Once revealed as the narrator, he speaks in much more scornful and condescending phrases when giving the preview.

Father's English voice actor also voiced Majhal from the 2003 anime.
In the 2009 anime, before Father gets sucked back to the Gate (while in his fourth appearance), he loses both of his arms (the other arm was lost when he destroyed Greed), unlike in the manga where he only loses one arm and one leg, ironically mirroring what happened to Ed at the beginning of the story.
In the original Japanese version of Brotherhood, when Father is in his "old man" form, he sounds like he is struggling to pronounce certain words, and talks at a very slow pace. When he is in his original form, he speaks much more fluently.
Truth's ultimate punishment for Father seems poetic: by letting the Eye of God drag him back into the Gate, they let Father become as high as God in some senses whilst stripping Father completely of the freedom he cherished.
In the manga, Father's eyes in his fourth form are dark purple and, like those of Envy, Lust and Greed, have slit pupils. In the 2009 anime, they are golden with round pupils like Edward's, possibly to further imitate Hohenheim's appearance. Also, Father's fourth appearance wears sandals in the manga whereas in the anime he appears barefoot.
Before meeting the Elric brothers for the first time, Father and Hohenheim's actions are coordinated very specifically to match up in the manga, leaving it ambiguous if he and Hohenheim are indeed the same person. Despite basing itself wholly on the manga, the 2009 anime does not include any of these scenes.
Since each of the homunculi he created were partially made of his soul in an attempt to cleanse himself of his sins, a part of him is still alive due to Pride never being fully destroyed.
The Gate that Father appears in front of is completely blank.
This could possibly signify that a homunculus cannot use alchemy normally.
Considering the gate seems to portray a different alchemic symbol (such as Mustang's transmutation circle) between alchemists, it may also be seen to represent Father's status as a being originating from the gate itself.
The gate could also represent real power or wisdom gained, as Truth stated that Father "stole his power from others", thus Father's gate being blank could also signify that he neither had any real power outside of his stolen souls nor ever learned anything.
When confronted by Truth after the final battle, Truth calls him such for not being able to truly understand the world and humans despite having not only the power to do so, but the time as well. Essentially, he never developed past being the Dwarf in the Flask in his mindset, something Hohenheim realizes prior and calls him out on. This is illustrated in his Gate of Truth: whereas every other Gate of Truth shown in the series has ornate designs on the door symbolizing what they know of alchemy and the world, Father is the only one who has blank doors.
